questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which constitutional amendment granted women the right to vote?

choices.text: 15th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 15th Amendment (1870) prohibited denying voting rights based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude — it applied to Black men, not women.

choices.text: 19th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 19th Amendment (1920) prohibits denying the right to vote based on sex, formally granting women's suffrage after decades of advocacy by the suffragette movement.

choices.text: 24th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 24th Amendment (1964) abolished poll taxes in federal elections — it expanded access for low-income voters but was not specifically about women's suffrage.

choices.text: 26th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 26th Amendment (1971) lowered the voting age from 21 to 18 — it addressed age-based voting restrictions, not sex-based ones.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following describes a closed primary election?

choices.text: Only registered members of a party may vote to select that party's candidates
choices.explanation: In a closed primary, participation is restricted to voters who are registered members of the party holding the primary — non-party members and independents cannot vote.

choices.text: Any registered voter may vote in either party's primary, regardless of party registration
choices.explanation: This describes an open primary, where voters can choose which party's primary to participate in regardless of their own party registration.

choices.text: Candidates from all parties appear on one ballot and the top two vote-getters advance
choices.explanation: This describes a blanket or jungle primary (used in California), where all candidates regardless of party appear on one ballot and the top two advance to the general election.

choices.text: Party leaders select the nominee through backroom negotiations rather than a public vote
choices.explanation: This describes a convention or caucus-dominated nomination system — a closed primary is still a public vote, just restricted to registered party members.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: The Electoral College requires a presidential candidate to win how many electoral votes to secure the presidency?

choices.explanation: With 538 total electoral votes (435 House + 100 Senate + 3 for D.C.), a candidate needs a majority — 270 — to win the presidency outright.
300 is not the threshold — the required majority of 538 electoral votes is 270. A candidate can win with exactly 270.
218 is the majority threshold in the House of Representatives, not the Electoral College. Electoral College majority is 270 of 538 votes.
51 would represent a majority of the 100-member Senate, not the Electoral College. Presidential elections require 270 electoral votes.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following is the primary function of a political party?

choices.text: To lobby Congress on behalf of specific industries
choices.explanation: Lobbying specific industries is the function of interest groups, not political parties. Interest groups advocate for narrow policy goals, while parties seek to win elections and control government.

choices.text: To nominate candidates, organize campaigns, and coordinate government action
choices.explanation: Political parties serve as linkage institutions that recruit and nominate candidates, develop platforms, mobilize voters, and coordinate action among elected officials to govern.

choices.text: To enforce campaign finance laws and regulate spending
choices.explanation: Campaign finance regulation is the function of the Federal Election Commission (FEC), an independent regulatory agency — not of political parties themselves.

choices.text: To provide balanced news coverage to the electorate
choices.explanation: Providing news coverage is the function of the media — political parties are advocacy organizations that seek to win elections and implement their platforms.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which term describes the practice of voting for candidates from different political parties in the same election?

choices.text: Retrospective voting
choices.explanation: Retrospective voting means evaluating candidates based on past performance (e.g., voting against an incumbent during bad economic times) — it refers to the basis for a vote, not splitting a ballot.

choices.text: Party-line voting
choices.explanation: Party-line voting means voting exclusively for candidates of one party — the opposite of split-ticket voting.

choices.text: Split-ticket voting
choices.explanation: Split-ticket voting occurs when a voter selects candidates from different parties in the same election — for example, voting for a Republican president and a Democratic senator.

choices.text: Rational choice voting
choices.explanation: Rational choice voting refers to voters making decisions based on careful cost-benefit calculations of each candidate's positions — it describes the motivation for voting, not the ballot pattern.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: What does voter turnout measure?

choices.text: The percentage of eligible voters who actually cast ballots in an election
choices.explanation: Voter turnout is calculated as the number of votes cast divided by the voting-eligible population (or voting-age population), expressed as a percentage.

choices.text: The total number of registered voters in the country
choices.explanation: Total registered voters is a count, not a rate — voter turnout is a percentage comparing votes cast to the eligible population, not an absolute number.

choices.text: The margin of victory in a given election
choices.explanation: Margin of victory measures the difference between the winner's and loser's vote totals — voter turnout measures participation rates, not electoral outcomes.

choices.text: The number of polling places available per registered voter
choices.explanation: The number of polling places per voter measures electoral infrastructure access, not voter turnout. Turnout specifically measures the percentage of eligible voters who participated.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: The winner-take-all system used in most U.S. presidential elections means that

choices.text: A candidate who wins a state's popular vote receives all of that state's electoral votes
choices.explanation: Under the winner-take-all (unit rule) system used in 48 states and D.C., whichever presidential candidate wins the plurality of a state's popular vote receives all of that state's electoral votes.

choices.text: Electoral votes are distributed proportionally based on each candidate's vote share
choices.explanation: Proportional allocation of electoral votes is used in Maine and Nebraska but not in most states — the winner-take-all system awards all electoral votes to the plurality winner.

choices.text: The candidate with the most total popular votes nationwide automatically wins
choices.explanation: The U.S. does not use a national popular vote — candidates can win the presidency while losing the national popular vote (as happened in 2000 and 2016) because of the state-by-state winner-take-all system.

choices.text: Third-party candidates receive electoral votes proportional to their national vote share
choices.explanation: Third-party candidates rarely win any electoral votes under the winner-take-all system because they must win a state's plurality to receive its electoral votes — a state-based proportional allocation does not exist.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following is the best definition of a Super PAC?

choices.text: A political party's official fundraising committee that is regulated by the FEC
choices.explanation: Party fundraising committees (like the DCCC and NRCC) are official party organizations with strict contribution limits — Super PACs operate independently of parties and have no contribution limits.

choices.text: An independent expenditure-only committee that can raise unlimited funds but cannot coordinate with campaigns
choices.explanation: Super PACs emerged after Citizens United (2010) and SpeechNow v. FEC — they may raise unlimited money from corporations, unions, and individuals, but may not contribute directly to or coordinate with any candidate's campaign.

choices.text: A government agency that enforces campaign finance laws
choices.explanation: Campaign finance laws are enforced by the Federal Election Commission (FEC), which is an independent regulatory agency — a Super PAC is a private organization that raises and spends money in elections.

choices.text: A nonprofit organization that can endorse candidates and make direct contributions to campaigns
choices.explanation: 501(c)(4) nonprofits can do political activity without disclosing donors, but they still cannot make direct contributions to campaigns — Super PACs also cannot make direct contributions but can spend unlimited amounts on independent expenditures.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following factors is most consistently associated with higher voter turnout?

choices.text: Higher levels of education
choices.explanation: Political science research consistently shows that education is the strongest predictor of voter turnout — more educated citizens have greater civic knowledge, political efficacy, and social networks that encourage participation.

choices.text: Living in a rural area
choices.explanation: Rural residents generally have lower voter turnout than suburban or urban residents — geographic isolation, fewer polling places, and lower average education levels contribute to lower participation rates.

choices.text: Being between ages 18 and 24
choices.explanation: Young adults ages 18-24 consistently have the lowest voter turnout of any age group — older citizens (especially 60+) have the highest turnout rates.

choices.text: Having a lower income
choices.explanation: Lower income is associated with lower voter turnout — barriers such as less flexible work schedules, transportation difficulties, and lower civic engagement all reduce participation among lower-income voters.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which constitutional amendment lowered the federal voting age from 21 to 18?

choices.text: 19th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 19th Amendment (1920) granted women the right to vote — it addressed sex discrimination in voting, not age restrictions.

choices.text: 24th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 24th Amendment (1964) abolished poll taxes in federal elections — it removed economic barriers to voting, not age restrictions.

choices.text: 26th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 26th Amendment (1971) lowered the voting age from 21 to 18, largely in response to the argument that young men being drafted to fight in Vietnam should be able to vote on the political leaders who sent them.

choices.text: 15th Amendment
choices.explanation: The 15th Amendment (1870) prohibited racial discrimination in voting rights — it was aimed at protecting Black male suffrage after the Civil War, not at changing the voting age.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was primarily designed to

choices.text: Lower the voting age to 18 for all elections
choices.explanation: Lowering the voting age was accomplished by the 26th Amendment in 1971 — the Voting Rights Act of 1965 specifically targeted racial discrimination in voting, especially in Southern states.

choices.text: Eliminate literacy tests and other barriers that disenfranchised Black voters in Southern states
choices.explanation: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 banned literacy tests, provided for federal oversight of elections in states with histories of discrimination, and established legal mechanisms to protect Black voting rights.

choices.text: Establish the Federal Election Commission to regulate campaign finance
choices.explanation: The FEC was established by the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 and its amendments — the Voting Rights Act was focused on eliminating racial barriers to voting access, not campaign finance.

choices.text: Require automatic voter registration in all states
choices.explanation: Automatic voter registration is a more recent state-level reform — the Voting Rights Act of 1965 focused on removing discriminatory barriers and enabling federal intervention in states suppressing minority voting.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: In the context of interest groups, the 'free rider problem' refers to

choices.text: Interest groups that receive government funding without lobbying Congress
choices.explanation: Government-funded groups are not the subject of the free rider problem — the concept refers to the tendency of individuals to benefit from collective goods without contributing to the effort of obtaining them.

choices.text: The tendency for individuals to benefit from a group's advocacy without joining or contributing to the group
choices.explanation: The free rider problem occurs because the policy benefits secured by interest groups (e.g., environmental protections, labor regulations) are public goods that benefit everyone, including those who did not join or fund the advocacy effort.

choices.text: Lobbyists who receive government contracts while working for interest groups
choices.explanation: Receiving government contracts while lobbying is related to the 'revolving door' problem — the free rider problem describes individual incentives to not join groups when their benefits are non-excludable.

choices.text: Political parties that benefit from interest group donations without endorsing the group's agenda
choices.explanation: This describes transactional dynamics between parties and donors — the free rider problem is specifically about individuals' incentives to not contribute to collective action when benefits are non-excludable.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content:
"We conclude that independent expenditures, including those made by corporations, do not give rise to corruption or the appearance of corruption... The Government may not suppress political speech on the basis of the speaker's corporate identity."
— Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010), majority opinion

questions.question: The Supreme Court's reasoning in Citizens United rested primarily on which constitutional principle?

choices.text: The Establishment Clause prevents government from favoring one group's speech over another's
choices.explanation: The Establishment Clause deals with government and religion — Citizens United was a First Amendment free speech case, not a religion case.

choices.text: Political spending constitutes protected speech under the First Amendment, which the government cannot restrict based on a speaker's identity
choices.explanation: Citizens United held that political spending is a form of protected speech and that the First Amendment prohibits Congress from restricting independent political expenditures based on corporate or union identity.

choices.text: The 14th Amendment's equal protection clause requires equal campaign spending rights for all entities
choices.explanation: The Court's reasoning was rooted in First Amendment free speech, not equal protection — the majority held that restrictions on corporate speech were unconstitutional content-based restrictions.

choices.text: Congress exceeded its commerce power when it regulated political speech in federal elections
choices.explanation: The challenge was on First Amendment free speech grounds, not Commerce Clause grounds — the Court did not question whether Congress had commerce authority but whether restrictions on political spending violated free speech.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which conclusion is best supported by the data in the table?

choices.text: Voter turnout has declined consistently in both presidential and midterm elections since 2010
choices.explanation: The data does not show a consistent decline — both presidential and midterm turnout increased over this period (2016 presidential: 55.7% vs 2020: 66.8%; 2014 midterm: 36.4% vs 2022: 46.8%).

choices.text: Presidential election years consistently produce higher voter turnout than midterm election years
choices.explanation: In every comparable period, presidential election turnout exceeds midterm turnout — presidential elections generate more attention, higher stakes, and stronger mobilization efforts that increase participation.

choices.text: Midterm elections in 2022 had higher turnout than the 2020 presidential election
choices.explanation: The 2022 midterm had 46.8% turnout compared to 66.8% in the 2020 presidential election — presidential elections consistently produce much higher turnout than midterms.

choices.text: Voter participation was identical in the 2018 and 2022 midterms
choices.explanation: The data shows 50.3% turnout in 2018 and 46.8% in 2022 — these are different figures, not identical.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "The Constitution does not mention political parties. Yet they have become central to American democracy... Two parties have dominated American politics for over 150 years, in large part because of the single-member plurality district system, which creates structural barriers for third parties."

questions.question: Which concept best explains why the passage argues the two-party system persists in the United States?

choices.text: Proportional representation rewards minor parties with legislative seats based on their share of the vote
choices.explanation: The passage argues the opposite — proportional representation would help third parties, but the U.S. uses single-member plurality districts which disadvantage them. The passage explains the two-party system persists because of structural barriers.

choices.text: Duverger's Law holds that single-member plurality systems tend to produce two dominant parties because third-party votes are 'wasted'
choices.explanation: Duverger's Law predicts that single-member plurality (winner-take-all) systems produce two dominant parties because rational voters fear 'wasting' votes on third-party candidates who cannot win seats, leading to consolidation around two major parties.

choices.text: The Constitution explicitly prohibits more than two political parties from competing in federal elections
choices.explanation: The passage itself notes the Constitution does not mention political parties — the two-party system is a structural outcome of electoral rules, not a constitutional prohibition.

choices.text: The Electoral College requires candidates to win a majority of electoral votes, eliminating third parties mathematically
choices.explanation: While the Electoral College does disadvantage third parties, the passage specifically cites single-member plurality districts as the primary structural cause — the Electoral College would be a contributing factor, not the only mechanism.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content:
"The Court holds today that money is not speech, and spending money to support a candidate is not protected political activity... [Dissent] The majority ignores two centuries of this Court's decisions holding that political speech may not be regulated based on the speaker's identity or wealth."
— Dissenting opinion paraphrase referencing Citizens United v. FEC

questions.question: According to the dissenting view in the passage above, the majority's error in Citizens United was that it

choices.text: Failed to apply the Establishment Clause to restrict corporate religious activities
choices.explanation: Citizens United involved the First Amendment's free speech protections, not the Establishment Clause — the dissent and majority debate centered on whether political spending constitutes protected speech.

choices.text: Incorrectly treated political spending as unprotected conduct rather than protected speech
choices.explanation: The passage presents a dissent arguing money is NOT speech — choice B describes the opposite error. The dissent in the passage claims the majority wrongly protected spending, so the error was treating spending as protected speech, not unprotected conduct.

choices.text: Expanded corporate rights without congressional authorization to do so
choices.explanation: Constitutional rights are not granted by Congress — the Court interprets the Constitution. The dissent's argument was about the proper scope of First Amendment speech protections, not congressional authorization.

choices.text: Overlooked precedents establishing that political spending by corporations can be limited to prevent corruption
choices.explanation: The actual dissent in Citizens United (written by Justice Stevens) argued the majority was wrong to overturn Austin v. Michigan Chamber of Commerce and other precedents that allowed Congress to limit corporate political spending to prevent corruption of the democratic process.

questions.subject: ap-government

title.text: 2020 Presidential Election Voter Turnout by Age Group

questions.question: Based on the chart, which of the following conclusions is best supported?

choices.text: Citizens over 65 have consistently lower turnout than those under 35 because they face more physical barriers to voting
choices.explanation: The chart shows the opposite — voters aged 65-74 had 76% turnout, far exceeding the 51% among 18-24-year-olds. Physical barriers would not explain lower turnout among young people.

choices.text: Voter turnout generally increases with age until the oldest cohort, suggesting civic engagement deepens over time
choices.explanation: The chart shows turnout rising from 51% (18-24) to 76% (65-74), with only a slight decline among voters 75+, consistent with research showing that civic engagement, habit formation, and higher education/income accumulate with age.

choices.text: The youngest and oldest voters have the same turnout rates, suggesting age is not a factor
choices.explanation: 18-24-year-olds had 51% turnout while 65-74-year-olds had 76% — a 25-point gap. This significant difference indicates age is an important factor in voter participation.

choices.text: All age groups over 55 have identical turnout rates because they have equal access to polling
choices.explanation: The chart shows variation among older voters (55-64: 72%, 65-74: 76%, 75+: 68%) — equal access to polling does not guarantee identical participation rates across all older cohorts.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'dealignment' in American politics?

choices.text: A permanent shift of voter groups from one party to another, creating a new majority coalition
choices.explanation: This describes realignment — a durable shift of coalition composition that creates a new dominant party. Dealignment is the weakening of party attachment overall, not a transfer to the other party.

choices.text: A weakening of party loyalty among voters, with increasing numbers identifying as independent
choices.explanation: Dealignment describes the gradual decline in strong party identification — voters increasingly identify as independents, split their tickets, and switch parties between elections rather than forming a new stable majority coalition.

choices.text: The process by which a minor party absorbs the voters of a major party after a critical election
choices.explanation: Minor parties rarely absorb major party voters — dealignment refers to voters leaving strong party identification and becoming independent, not migrating to third parties.

choices.text: The constitutional process for removing a political party from the ballot
choices.explanation: There is no constitutional process for removing parties from ballots — dealignment is a sociological concept describing declining voter identification with political parties, not a legal or electoral mechanism.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Horse-race journalism focuses on who is winning or losing the election rather than on substantive policy issues. Critics argue this coverage leads voters to make decisions based on momentum and perceived electability rather than candidates' actual policy positions."

questions.question: Based on the passage, horse-race journalism most directly threatens which aspect of democratic governance?

choices.text: The ability of the press to serve as a watchdog against government corruption
choices.explanation: Watchdog journalism focuses on investigating government misconduct — horse-race journalism reduces substantive policy coverage, which is a different dysfunction than failing to expose corruption.

choices.text: Informed citizen decision-making based on policy substance rather than competitive dynamics
choices.explanation: The passage directly argues that horse-race coverage diverts attention from policy substance — this undermines the informed electorate that democracy requires, as voters focus on who is 'winning' rather than evaluating candidates on their actual positions.

choices.text: The government's ability to set the media agenda through press releases and briefings
choices.explanation: Government agenda-setting relates to priming and framing effects on media coverage — horse-race journalism is a critique of how media covers elections, not a concern about government manipulation of the press.

choices.text: The First Amendment protection of political speech in press coverage
choices.explanation: Horse-race journalism is constitutionally protected speech — the passage's critique is that this type of coverage, while legal, undermines the quality of democratic deliberation, not that it violates First Amendment principles.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best explains the 'incumbency advantage' in congressional elections?

choices.text: Incumbents benefit from name recognition, the franking privilege, case work, and access to large donor networks
choices.explanation: Congressional incumbents enjoy structural advantages including free mail to constituents (franking privilege), staff resources for casework that builds goodwill, established donor networks, and the public recognition that comes from years of serving in office.

choices.text: Congressional rules require incumbents to receive 5% more funding than challengers from the FEC
choices.explanation: The FEC does not allocate extra funding to incumbents — the incumbency advantage comes from structural and informal factors, not from regulatory favoritism.

choices.text: Gerrymandering automatically protects all incumbents from competitive elections
choices.explanation: Gerrymandering can protect incumbents through partisan mapmaking, but it does not 'automatically' protect all incumbents — primary challenges, demographic change, and wave elections can still defeat incumbents even in favorable districts.

choices.text: The Constitution guarantees sitting members of Congress the right to run for reelection without term limits
choices.explanation: While Congress lacks term limits, this is a structural feature, not an incumbency 'advantage' — the incumbency advantage refers to the practical benefits incumbents have over challengers in raising money and gaining voter recognition.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which pattern does the table best illustrate about how different interest group types use different lobbying strategies?

choices.text: All interest groups rely exclusively on PAC donations as their primary influence strategy
choices.explanation: The table shows varied strategies — the NAACP and ACLU focus primarily on litigation and organizing, not PAC donations. Different group types use strategies suited to their resources and goals.

choices.text: Groups with large budgets consistently choose litigation over lobbying as their primary strategy
choices.explanation: The table does not provide budget information, and it shows business groups with large resources using direct lobbying — litigation is used by public interest and ideological groups, not necessarily the wealthiest.

choices.text: Different interest group types employ strategies that reflect their organizational strengths, resources, and policy goals
choices.explanation: The table illustrates how business groups leverage financial resources for direct lobbying and PAC donations, labor unions use membership-based grassroots power, and public/ideological groups rely on litigation and organizing — each strategy reflects the group's comparative advantage.

choices.text: Public interest groups are more effective than corporate groups because they use legal strategies
choices.explanation: The table does not provide effectiveness data — it shows different strategies, not their relative effectiveness. Effectiveness depends on context, resources, and political environment.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes 'agenda setting' as a media effect?

choices.text: The media's ability to change voter preferences by directly advocating for specific candidates
choices.explanation: Direct advocacy that changes voter preferences describes 'persuasion' — agenda setting is the subtler effect of determining which issues receive public attention, not telling people what to think about those issues.

choices.text: The government's power to control what topics the media covers through press conferences
choices.explanation: Government attempts to control media coverage relate to media management strategies — agenda setting refers to the media's own power to determine which issues the public considers important, not government control of the press.

choices.text: The media's power to influence which issues the public considers important by choosing what to cover prominently
choices.explanation: Agenda setting (McCombs and Shaw) holds that the media does not tell people what to think but tells them what to think about — by prominently covering certain issues, the media elevates their importance in public consciousness.

choices.text: The ability of interest groups to purchase advertising time to place their preferred issues in front of voters
choices.explanation: Interest group advertising is a form of paid media — agenda setting describes the editorial choices of news organizations that determine which issues receive news coverage, independent of advertising.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Baker v. Carr (1962) held that federal courts could hear challenges to state legislative apportionment, overturning the 'political question' doctrine that had previously kept such cases out of federal courts. This decision opened the door for Reynolds v. Sims (1964), which established the 'one person, one vote' principle."

questions.question: The significance of Baker v. Carr was that it

choices.text: Directly established that each legislative district must have equal population
choices.explanation: Equal population requirements came from Reynolds v. Sims (1964), the follow-on case the passage mentions — Baker v. Carr only opened the courthouse door by declaring redistricting cases justiciable.

choices.text: Made redistricting cases justiciable, allowing federal courts to review legislative apportionment
choices.explanation: Baker v. Carr's primary holding was that challenges to state legislative apportionment are justiciable — political questions that courts can address, clearing the way for subsequent 'one person, one vote' rulings.

choices.text: Required states to adopt independent redistricting commissions to draw congressional maps
choices.explanation: Independent redistricting commissions are a state-level reform — Baker v. Carr only made redistricting justiciable and did not mandate any particular method for drawing districts.

choices.text: Prohibited racial gerrymandering by requiring that race not be the predominant factor in drawing districts
choices.explanation: Prohibiting racial gerrymandering was the holding of Shaw v. Reno (1993) — Baker v. Carr was about whether courts could hear apportionment cases at all, not about the standards for drawing districts.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which model of voting behavior suggests voters choose candidates whose policy positions are closest to their own?

choices.text: Retrospective voting model
choices.explanation: Retrospective voting means evaluating candidates based on past performance (e.g., economic conditions) rather than comparing policy positions — it looks backward, not at prospective policy positions.

choices.text: Party-line voting model
choices.explanation: Party-line voting means voting exclusively for one party's candidates regardless of their specific positions — it does not require comparing individual policy positions to one's own.

choices.text: Prospective (issue) voting model
choices.explanation: Prospective or issue voting holds that voters choose candidates based on future policy promises and platform positions that are closest to the voter's own preferences — also related to the spatial model of voting.

choices.text: Valence voting model
choices.explanation: Valence voting focuses on candidate traits, competence, and consensus issues (e.g., economic prosperity) rather than specific policy proximity — voters evaluate overall performance on widely shared goals rather than selecting the closest policy position.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "The Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA) of 2002 banned 'soft money' — unlimited contributions to political parties — and restricted issue advocacy ads in the 60 days before a general election. In Citizens United (2010), the Supreme Court struck down restrictions on independent expenditures by corporations and unions, dramatically expanding the role of money in elections."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the tension between campaign finance restrictions and First Amendment protections?

choices.text: Campaign finance laws violate the 5th Amendment because they take property (money) without just compensation
choices.explanation: Campaign finance challenges have been based on First Amendment free speech grounds, not 5th Amendment takings — the Court has never held that campaign finance restrictions constitute an unconstitutional taking.

choices.text: The Court has held that while some limits on direct contributions are permissible, unlimited restrictions on independent political expenditures violate free speech
choices.explanation: The Court has distinguished between direct contributions to candidates (which can be limited under Buckley v. Valeo to prevent corruption) and independent expenditures (which Citizens United held cannot be limited because they do not give rise to corruption or its appearance).

choices.text: BCRA was ruled entirely unconstitutional by the Supreme Court in 2002, preventing any campaign finance regulation
choices.explanation: BCRA (McCain-Feingold) was largely upheld initially — Citizens United in 2010 struck down only certain spending restrictions on corporations and unions, not the entire law. Contribution limits and disclosure requirements remain in place.

choices.text: The First Amendment applies only to individual citizens, not to corporations or political parties
choices.explanation: Citizens United explicitly rejected this argument — the Court held that the First Amendment's text and history do not support limiting its protections based on the identity of the speaker, including corporations.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Baker v. Carr (1962) established that federal courts have jurisdiction over legislative apportionment challenges... The 'one person, one vote' standard established in Reynolds v. Sims (1964) requires that state legislative districts be drawn with substantially equal populations."

questions.question: Which of the following best describes how Baker v. Carr changed the relationship between the courts and state legislatures?

choices.text: It required all states to immediately redraw their legislative maps to comply with equal population standards
choices.explanation: Baker v. Carr did not set an equal population standard — that came from Reynolds v. Sims (1964). Baker v. Carr only made redistricting challenges justiciable, leaving the equal population standard to later cases.

choices.text: It removed the political question doctrine barrier, allowing federal courts to review and potentially invalidate malapportioned legislative districts
choices.explanation: Prior to Baker v. Carr, courts refused to hear redistricting cases as 'political questions' beyond judicial authority. The ruling established justiciability, fundamentally expanding federal court power over state legislative processes.

choices.text: It transferred all redistricting authority from state legislatures to the federal judiciary
choices.explanation: Baker v. Carr did not transfer redistricting power to courts — states still draw their own maps. Courts gained the power to review those maps for constitutional compliance, not to draw districts themselves.

choices.text: It applied only to congressional district maps and had no bearing on state legislative apportionment
choices.explanation: Baker v. Carr specifically concerned Tennessee's state legislative apportionment — while it opened the door for congressional district cases as well, its direct holding was about state legislative maps, not exclusively congressional ones.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Political realignment occurs when a significant portion of the electorate permanently shifts its party allegiance, fundamentally altering the composition of the two parties and creating a new dominant coalition. The New Deal coalition of 1932 — uniting urban workers, ethnic minorities, Southern whites, and liberals — is a classic example."

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would best represent a political realignment, as described in the passage?

choices.text: A sitting president loses a reelection bid due to economic recession but the party remains dominant at the congressional level
choices.explanation: This describes a short-term electoral setback — a single election loss does not constitute realignment because it does not reflect a durable, lasting shift in the party coalitions or voting patterns.

choices.text: A third-party candidate wins one presidential election but the party system returns to its prior configuration in subsequent elections
choices.explanation: A temporary third-party disruption is not realignment — realignment requires a durable shift to a new majority coalition, not a one-time electoral disruption that reverts to the prior pattern.

choices.text: White working-class voters who previously supported Democrats consistently shift to Republicans across multiple election cycles, creating a new Republican coalition
choices.explanation: This describes realignment — a durable shift of a significant voter group (white working-class) from one party to another that persists across multiple elections and restructures the parties' coalitions, consistent with the passage's definition.

choices.text: Voter turnout drops significantly in a midterm election, with both parties' vote shares remaining proportionally stable
choices.explanation: Lower turnout with stable party proportions describes a turnout problem, not realignment — realignment requires a shift in which groups identify with each party, not just a change in overall participation levels.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why third parties rarely win elections in the United States but still influence the political system?

choices.text: Third parties are constitutionally prohibited from winning federal offices but can hold state-level positions
choices.explanation: No constitutional provision prohibits third parties from winning federal office — the barriers are structural (winner-take-all elections, ballot access laws) and behavioral (rational voting), not constitutional.

choices.text: Third parties cannot raise money under campaign finance law, making it impossible to compete with major parties
choices.explanation: Third parties can legally raise campaign funds — the barriers to third-party success are structural (single-member plurality system, ballot access requirements) and public behavior (spoiler concerns), not legal prohibitions on fundraising.

choices.text: Winner-take-all electoral rules punish vote-splitting while third parties influence policy by pulling major parties toward their positions and serving as spoilers
choices.explanation: The single-member plurality system means third-party votes rarely translate into seats (Duverger's Law), but third parties shape elections by acting as spoilers (e.g., Nader in 2000) and force major parties to adopt popular positions to absorb their support.

choices.text: Third parties lack any influence because voters immediately abandon them once they realize their votes will be wasted
choices.explanation: Third parties do influence politics through spoiler effects and platform influence — the historical record shows third-party candidates have affected election outcomes (1912 Bull Moose, 2000 Nader) even without winning themselves.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Based on the table, which of the following conclusions about third-party candidates is best supported?

choices.text: Third-party candidates consistently win over 10% of the popular vote in recent presidential elections
choices.explanation: Only 1992 shows a third-party share above 10% (Perot's 18.9%) — the other years show third-party shares well below 10%, making 'consistently' inaccurate.

choices.text: Third-party vote shares are highest when a strong independent candidate with significant name recognition and resources enters the race
choices.explanation: Ross Perot's unprecedented 18.9% in 1992 and 8.4% in 1996 correlate with his billionaire-funded self-financed campaign and high name recognition — other years with lesser-known third-party candidates show much lower percentages.

choices.text: The Democratic Party always benefits when third-party candidates receive higher vote shares
choices.explanation: The effect depends on which voters the third-party candidate draws from — in 1992, analysts debated whether Perot hurt Bush or Clinton. Third-party spoiler effects are context-dependent, not systematically beneficial to one party.

choices.text: Third-party candidates have received more popular votes in recent decades than in earlier U.S. history
choices.explanation: The table only covers 1992-2020 and cannot support comparisons to earlier history. Historically, 1912 (Theodore Roosevelt's 27.4% as Progressive Party) significantly exceeds any figure in this table.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Section 5 of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 originally required states and localities with histories of voting discrimination to obtain federal 'preclearance' before changing voting laws. In Shelby County v. Holder (2013), the Supreme Court struck down the coverage formula, effectively gutting the preclearance requirement."

questions.question: Which constitutional tension does Shelby County v. Holder (2013) best illustrate?

choices.text: The tension between the Establishment Clause and Congress's power to enforce civil rights legislation
choices.explanation: Shelby County involved federalism and the 15th Amendment's enforcement provisions — the Establishment Clause (religion) was not at issue in this voting rights case.

choices.text: The tension between the 10th Amendment's protection of state sovereignty over elections and Congress's 15th Amendment power to enforce equal voting rights
choices.explanation: Shelby County illustrates the federalism tension between state control over election administration (10th Amendment reserved powers) and Congress's broad 15th Amendment enforcement power to combat racial discrimination in voting — the Court held the coverage formula violated state sovereignty principles.

choices.text: The tension between the right to bear arms and Congress's power to regulate federal elections
choices.explanation: The 2nd Amendment right to bear arms had no relevance to Shelby County — the case was about voting rights, congressional enforcement of the 15th Amendment, and state sovereignty over election administration.

choices.text: The tension between executive power and congressional authority to determine voter eligibility requirements
choices.explanation: Shelby County was a challenge to congressional legislation (the VRA) not executive overreach — the dispute was between Congress's enforcement power under the 15th Amendment and state sovereignty over elections, not a separation of powers conflict.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'revolving door' problem in the relationship between interest groups and the federal government?

choices.text: The tendency of interest group lobbyists to switch which party they support based on which party controls Congress
choices.explanation: Switching party allegiance describes bipartisan lobbying strategy, not the revolving door — the revolving door specifically refers to personnel movement between government and the private sector/lobbying.

choices.text: The movement of individuals between government positions and interest group or lobbying jobs, creating potential conflicts of interest
choices.explanation: The revolving door describes the pattern where government officials leave to become lobbyists (using their connections and knowledge) and lobbyists enter government — this creates potential conflicts of interest and regulatory capture because of personal relationships and shared career interests.

choices.text: The cyclical nature of interest group influence that peaks before elections and declines after
choices.explanation: The cyclical rise and fall of influence around elections describes electoral lobbying patterns — the revolving door is specifically about personnel movement between government and the private sector/lobbying industry.

choices.text: The process by which interest groups are created, peak in influence, and then dissolve when their policy goals are achieved
choices.explanation: This describes a group's lifecycle, not the revolving door — the revolving door is a structural feature of the relationship between government and interest groups created by personnel movement, not group dissolution.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "In modern campaigns, candidates rely heavily on data analytics to identify and target likely supporters for voter mobilization. Microtargeting allows campaigns to send tailored messages to specific demographic groups based on consumer data, social media behavior, and voter file information."

questions.question: Which democratic value is most directly challenged by the microtargeting described in the passage?

choices.text: Federalism, because microtargeting allows national campaigns to interfere with state-level elections
choices.explanation: Microtargeting is used by all types of campaigns including state-level ones — it does not specifically implicate federalism. The democratic concern is about information manipulation and civic deliberation.

choices.text: Separation of powers, because data analytics allows the executive branch to manipulate legislative elections
choices.explanation: Microtargeting is used by campaigns of all types, not specifically by the executive branch — the concern is about democratic deliberation and equal information access, not separation of powers.

choices.text: An informed and equal public sphere, because citizens receive different, personalized political messages rather than engaging with shared information
choices.explanation: Microtargeting fragments the public information environment — different voters see different tailored messages rather than engaging with common information. This challenges deliberative democracy by undermining the shared informational basis for civic debate.

choices.text: The right to vote, because microtargeting can be used to suppress the votes of targeted demographic groups
choices.explanation: While voter suppression is a real concern, the passage specifically describes microtargeting for voter mobilization (turning out supporters) — the passage's democratic challenge is about personalized messaging fragmenting shared information, not vote suppression.

questions.subject: ap-government

rows: College graduates vote at ~80% vs ~45% for non-HS grads
Top income quartile votes 30+ points more than bottom quartile
Peak voting age: habitual voters established
~3-5% turnout increase in states with SDR
Some studies show 2-3% reduction, especially among minorities

questions.question: Based on the table, which of the following policy recommendations is best supported by the evidence presented?

choices.text: Eliminating voter ID requirements would be the most effective single reform to increase voter turnout
choices.explanation: The table shows voter ID laws have only a 'modest negative effect, disputed' — education level and income have far stronger effects on turnout. Eliminating ID requirements alone would not be the 'most effective' reform.

choices.text: Civic education programs targeting young people could have the greatest long-term impact on turnout by building the habits and knowledge associated with higher turnout
choices.explanation: The table shows education has the strongest positive effect on turnout — targeting education investments toward young voters, who have the lowest current turnout, would build the civic habits and knowledge base most associated with high participation rates.

choices.text: Same-day registration is the single most impactful reform since it directly removes a structural barrier
choices.explanation: The table shows same-day registration has only a 'modest positive effect' of 3-5% — education and income effects are much larger. Structural reforms help but are not more impactful than the socioeconomic factors shown in the table.

choices.text: Since income is the strongest predictor of turnout, wealth redistribution is the most effective electoral policy
choices.explanation: While income correlates with turnout, the table also shows education is equally or more important — and wealth redistribution is a broad economic policy, not specifically a voting-access policy. The evidence supports multiple intersecting factors, not a single cause.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes 'prospective voting'?

choices.text: Voting based on a candidate's past performance and record in office
choices.explanation: Voting based on past performance is retrospective voting — prospective voting looks forward to what candidates promise to do, not backward at what they have done.

choices.text: Voting based on which candidates have the most campaign money to spend
choices.explanation: Campaign spending is not the defining factor in prospective voting — prospective voters evaluate candidates based on their policy proposals and platforms for the future, not their fundraising prowess.

choices.text: Voting based on candidates' future policy promises and platforms
choices.explanation: Prospective voting means choosing candidates based on their future-oriented policy promises — voters evaluate party platforms, campaign promises, and what candidates say they will do if elected, rather than past performance.

choices.text: Voting based on the candidate who most closely shares the voter's demographic characteristics
choices.explanation: Voting based on demographic similarity is called 'descriptive representation' or identity voting — prospective voting is about policy positions and future-oriented promises, not demographic matching.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "The media's role in democracy has been described as a 'fourth estate' — an unofficial branch of government that serves as a watchdog, holding the government accountable to the public. However, the rise of partisan media and social media echo chambers has led critics to question whether the press still fulfills this function."

questions.question: According to the passage, what is the primary concern about modern media's ability to serve as a 'fourth estate'?

choices.text: The government has seized control of major news networks, undermining editorial independence
choices.explanation: The passage does not claim government control of media — it identifies partisan orientation and echo chambers as the challenge to the watchdog function, not government ownership or censorship.

choices.text: Partisan media and social media echo chambers may prevent the press from providing the objective accountability needed to check government power
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states that partisan media and echo chambers have 'led critics to question' whether the press still serves its watchdog function — the concern is that partisan alignment undermines the non-partisan accountability role of a free press.

choices.text: The First Amendment no longer protects press freedom in the digital age, leaving journalists vulnerable to government censorship
choices.explanation: The passage does not suggest First Amendment protections have weakened — the concern is about media behavior (partisan orientation) and audience behavior (echo chambers), not legal protection for journalists.

choices.text: Social media companies have replaced traditional journalism, and they are not subject to the same First Amendment standards
choices.explanation: While social media's role is relevant, the passage's concern is specifically about whether media outlets (traditional and digital) can provide objective accountability — not about differential First Amendment treatment of platforms.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes 'grassroots lobbying' as a strategy used by interest groups?

choices.text: Professional lobbyists meeting directly with members of Congress to influence legislation
choices.explanation: Direct meetings between professional lobbyists and legislators describe 'direct lobbying' or 'inside lobbying' — grassroots lobbying involves mobilizing ordinary citizens, not professional advocates.

choices.text: Interest groups funding political campaigns through Super PAC contributions
choices.explanation: Funding campaigns through Super PACs is electoral lobbying — grassroots lobbying involves organizing and mobilizing constituents to contact their representatives, not campaign donations.

choices.text: Interest groups mobilizing their members and the public to contact legislators, sign petitions, or attend rallies to pressure decision-makers
choices.explanation: Grassroots lobbying (or 'outside lobbying') involves organizing ordinary citizens and members to communicate directly with their elected officials — through calls, letters, petitions, and demonstrations — creating political pressure from the bottom up.

choices.text: Filing lawsuits in federal court to challenge legislation that harms the group's interests
choices.explanation: Filing lawsuits is litigation strategy — a different approach used by some groups (especially civil rights and public interest organizations). Grassroots lobbying specifically refers to citizen mobilization to influence legislators.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "The Electoral College was designed by the Founders to balance the interests of large and small states, provide a buffer between popular passion and presidential selection, and give states a central role in the process. Critics argue it has become an anti-democratic anachronism that allows a candidate to win the presidency while losing the national popular vote."

questions.question: The passage identifies which tension at the heart of the Electoral College debate?

choices.text: The tension between the Founders' original intent to limit democracy and modern expectations of direct popular representation
choices.explanation: The passage contrasts the Founders' deliberate design (buffering passion, protecting state interests) with contemporary democratic expectations (the president should reflect the popular will) — this is the core tension between the original design and modern democratic norms.

choices.text: The tension between the power of Congress and the power of the president in election administration
choices.explanation: The passage discusses the Electoral College's anti-democratic potential and original design, not a conflict between Congress and the president over election administration.

choices.text: The tension between large-state and small-state interests in presidential elections
choices.explanation: The passage mentions the large/small state balance as part of the original design rationale, but the primary tension identified is between that design and modern democratic expectations — the anti-democratic outcome of popular vote losers winning the presidency.

choices.text: The tension between the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause and the unequal weight of electoral votes
choices.explanation: While equal protection challenges to the Electoral College exist, the passage does not frame the debate in 14th Amendment terms — it frames it as original design versus modern democratic expectations.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes 'political efficacy'?

choices.text: The belief that one's political actions can influence government decisions
choices.explanation: Political efficacy refers to citizens' belief that they can understand and effectively influence the political system — internal efficacy (ability to understand) and external efficacy (belief the system is responsive) together predict greater political participation.

choices.text: The efficiency with which a government makes and implements policy decisions
choices.explanation: Government efficiency describes institutional capacity for policy implementation — political efficacy is a psychological attribute of citizens (their belief in their own ability to influence government), not a measure of government performance.

choices.text: The ability of a political party to win elections consistently over time
choices.explanation: Consistent electoral success describes electoral dominance — political efficacy refers to individual citizens' beliefs about their capacity to influence government, not a party's competitive strength.

choices.text: The effectiveness of lobbying campaigns in changing legislative outcomes
choices.explanation: Lobbying effectiveness measures interest group influence — political efficacy is an individual psychological concept about whether citizens believe their participation matters, not a measure of organized group success.

questions.subject: ap-government

rows: Any registered voter, all candidates one ballot
Lowest — top two advance regardless of party

questions.question: Based on the table, which of the following conclusions about primary election systems is best supported?

choices.text: Closed primaries produce the most moderate nominees because more voters participate
choices.explanation: The table shows the opposite — closed primaries give parties 'high control' and exclude moderates, tending to produce more ideologically extreme nominees who appeal to the party base rather than the broader electorate.

choices.text: Open primaries reduce party control over nominations, potentially producing more moderate nominees who appeal beyond the party base
choices.explanation: The table shows open primaries 'allow cross-party voting,' reducing party control and allowing independent and opposition voters to participate — this creates incentives for candidates to appeal beyond the ideological party base toward the median voter.

choices.text: Blanket primaries guarantee that two candidates from different parties always advance to the general election
choices.explanation: The table notes the top two candidates advance 'regardless of party' — it is possible for two candidates from the same party to advance if they finish first and second in a blanket primary, as has occurred in California.

choices.text: Closed primaries have the highest voter turnout because party members are more motivated to participate
choices.explanation: The table shows closed primaries have 'lower overall' turnout compared to open and blanket primaries — restricting participation to registered party members limits the pool of potential voters.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "The 'gender gap' in American politics refers to the consistent difference in political preferences and voting behavior between men and women. Since 1980, women have consistently voted at higher rates for Democratic presidential candidates than men, with gaps of 7-12 percentage points in recent elections."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the gender gap is significant for understanding American elections?

choices.text: It demonstrates that women and men have identical policy preferences but vote differently due to candidate personality factors
choices.explanation: The passage indicates consistent partisan differences, not identical policy preferences — research shows systematic differences in policy priorities (e.g., social welfare, healthcare) between men and women that contribute to the gap.

choices.text: It shows that women are better-informed voters than men and therefore make more rational electoral choices
choices.explanation: The gender gap reflects different partisan preferences, not differential information levels — research does not support the claim that one gender is 'more rational' in electoral decision-making.

choices.text: It demonstrates that gender shapes political socialization and policy preferences in ways that produce systematic partisan differences between men and women
choices.explanation: The consistent 7-12 point gender gap indicates that gender is a significant agent of political socialization producing systematic partisan differences — it shows that demographic factors shape political behavior and party coalition composition.

choices.text: It proves that women are more likely to be single-issue voters focused exclusively on reproductive rights
choices.explanation: The gender gap encompasses a broad range of issues beyond reproductive rights — research shows differences in priorities around economic security, gun control, and social programs. Reducing it to a single issue oversimplifies a complex phenomenon.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'framing effect' in media coverage?

choices.text: The media's choice of which stories to cover and which to ignore
choices.explanation: Choosing which stories to cover describes agenda setting — framing refers to how a story is presented once it is covered, specifically the interpretive frame used to contextualize events.

choices.text: The way media presents information that influences how audiences interpret and evaluate issues
choices.explanation: Framing refers to how the media packages information — the same event can be framed as a 'crime problem' or an 'inequality problem,' and the chosen frame shapes how audiences think about causes, consequences, and solutions.

choices.text: The tendency of media to make voters care more about recent events than historical ones
choices.explanation: Recency bias in political memory relates to different psychological processes — framing specifically refers to interpretive packages and contextual presentations that shape how issues are understood, not temporal bias.

choices.text: The process by which candidates use debate performance to shape voter perceptions of their competence
choices.explanation: Candidate debate strategy is a form of candidate communication — media framing refers specifically to how journalists and news organizations contextualize and present events to audiences, shaping their interpretive lens.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of a 'linkage institution' in American democracy?

choices.text: The Supreme Court
choices.explanation: The Supreme Court is a governmental institution that makes legal decisions — linkage institutions connect citizens to government through political participation, not through judicial rulings.

choices.text: The Federal Reserve
choices.explanation: The Federal Reserve is an independent monetary policy agency of the government — linkage institutions connect citizen preferences to the political process, which monetary policy agencies do not do.

choices.text: A political party
choices.explanation: Linkage institutions connect citizens to government by translating public preferences into political action — political parties are classic linkage institutions because they recruit candidates, mobilize voters, organize government, and bridge citizen preferences and government decisions.

choices.text: The Cabinet
choices.explanation: The Cabinet is an executive branch institution composed of department heads appointed by the president — it is a governmental decision-making body, not a linkage institution connecting citizens to government.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Rational choice theory suggests that citizens should rarely vote because the probability that any one vote will be decisive is vanishingly small, making the expected benefit of voting negligible compared to the costs (time, information gathering). Yet most democracies maintain significant voter turnout — the so-called 'paradox of voting.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best resolves the 'paradox of voting' described in the passage?

choices.text: Voters are irrational and do not consider the costs and benefits of voting before going to the polls
choices.explanation: Simply asserting irrationality does not resolve the paradox — political scientists have identified multiple rational explanations for voting (civic duty, expressive voting, group solidarity) that don't require abandoning rational choice theory entirely.

choices.text: Citizens vote because they derive utility from the act of participating itself — through civic duty, group solidarity, and expressive identity — independent of the electoral outcome
choices.explanation: The most widely accepted resolution to the paradox is that voters receive benefits from participation itself (not just from changing the outcome) — these include satisfying a sense of civic duty, expressing group solidarity, and affirming their political identity, which makes voting rational even when one vote is unlikely to be decisive.

choices.text: Voter turnout would be zero if citizens were truly rational, so any positive turnout proves rational choice theory is wrong
choices.explanation: Rather than abandoning rational choice theory, scholars have expanded it to include non-outcome utilities (civic duty, expressive benefits) — the paradox is a puzzle within rational choice theory that has been addressed without rejecting the framework.

choices.text: Most voters do not calculate costs and benefits because they lack the mathematical ability to do so
choices.explanation: The resolution to the paradox is not about cognitive limitations but about the different types of utility voters receive — civic duty and expressive benefits are rational motivations that do not require calculating electoral probabilities.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "The iron triangle describes the mutually reinforcing relationship among a congressional subcommittee, an executive agency, and an interest group operating in a specific policy area. Each actor benefits from the arrangement: the agency gets appropriations, the interest group gets favorable regulation, and the committee members get electoral support and campaign contributions."

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios best illustrates an 'iron triangle' as described in the passage?

choices.text: The Senate Armed Services Committee, the Department of Defense, and defense contractors mutually supporting high defense budgets and favorable procurement policies
choices.explanation: This is the textbook iron triangle example — the Senate Armed Services Committee controls defense appropriations, the DoD needs those appropriations, and defense contractors provide campaign contributions and constituent jobs, with all three actors reinforcing each other's interests.

choices.text: The President, the House Speaker, and the Chief Justice negotiating a compromise on a major piece of legislation
choices.explanation: This describes inter-branch negotiation among the three formal branches of government — an iron triangle involves a subcommittee, an executive agency, and a private interest group, not the three constitutional branches' top leaders.

choices.text: Two senators of opposing parties compromising on bipartisan legislation that satisfies neither party's base
choices.explanation: Bipartisan legislative compromise is a legislative process, not an iron triangle — iron triangles involve private interest groups, not just legislators, and they typically represent policy capture rather than democratic compromise.

choices.text: A federal court striking down an agency regulation as exceeding its statutory authority
choices.explanation: Judicial review of agency action is a check on the executive branch — an iron triangle is a mutually beneficial relationship among a congressional committee, agency, and interest group, which the courts would be checking rather than participating in.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Scholars debate whether the United States is better characterized as a pluralist democracy — where many competing groups influence policy — or an elite democracy — where a small, wealthy, and interconnected elite effectively determines policy outcomes, regardless of public preferences."

questions.question: Which empirical finding would most strongly support the elite theory of democracy over the pluralist theory?

choices.text: Interest groups with diverse memberships and modest budgets successfully block corporate-backed legislation on a regular basis
choices.explanation: Regular success by non-elite groups would support pluralism — elite theory would predict that well-resourced elite groups consistently prevail over less-wealthy counterparts, not that diverse groups successfully challenge elite interests.

choices.text: Policy outcomes correlate strongly with the preferences of the wealthy but not with the preferences of the average citizen
choices.explanation: This finding — from research such as Gilens and Page (2014) — directly supports elite theory: if policy tracks elite preferences but not median voter preferences, it suggests the democratic system is more responsive to concentrated wealth than to the public broadly.

choices.text: Multiple competing interest groups regularly reach compromises in the legislative process through negotiation
choices.explanation: Regular compromise among competing groups describes pluralism — elite theory would predict that one wealthy elite group or class ultimately prevails, not that diverse groups negotiate equal compromises.

choices.text: Voter turnout is higher in presidential elections than in midterm elections
choices.explanation: Differential turnout between election types is a behavioral pattern unrelated to the elite vs. pluralist theory debate — it does not speak to whether policy outcomes track elite or median preferences.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: The Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA) of 2002 attempted to address which campaign finance concern?

choices.text: The proliferation of Super PACs making unlimited independent expenditures after Citizens United
choices.explanation: Super PACs were created after Citizens United v. FEC (2010), which came after BCRA — BCRA could not have addressed a problem that did not yet exist. BCRA focused on soft money and issue ads.

choices.text: The unlimited contributions to political parties ('soft money') and issue advocacy ads close to elections that circumvented contribution limits
choices.explanation: BCRA (McCain-Feingold) banned unlimited 'soft money' contributions to national parties and restricted 'electioneering communications' (issue ads naming candidates) in the 60 days before a general election and 30 days before a primary.

choices.text: The lack of federal disclosure requirements for campaign donors contributing less than $200
choices.explanation: Small donor disclosure thresholds were not BCRA's primary focus — BCRA targeted unlimited soft money contributions to parties and issue ads near election day, not the threshold for small donor disclosure requirements.

choices.text: The use of foreign money by non-citizens to influence U.S. federal elections
choices.explanation: Foreign money in U.S. elections was already prohibited under prior law — BCRA focused on the soft money loophole and issue advertising that allowed unlimited domestic money to influence elections outside of direct contribution limits.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "The 2010 Citizens United and 2010 SpeechNow decisions, combined with subsequent FEC rulemaking, enabled the creation of Super PACs — organizations that can accept unlimited contributions from corporations, unions, and individuals for independent expenditures. By 2020, Super PACs had spent over $3 billion in federal elections."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the Supreme Court's reasoning in Citizens United was controversial from a democratic theory perspective?

choices.text: Critics argued it violated the Establishment Clause by allowing religious organizations to spend unlimited money in elections
choices.explanation: The Establishment Clause deals with government and religion — the democratic theory critique of Citizens United was about the role of concentrated wealth in elections, not religious organizations' involvement.

choices.text: Critics argued that equating money with speech effectively gives wealthy individuals and corporations greater political influence than ordinary citizens, undermining political equality
choices.explanation: The core democratic theory critique is that if spending money is protected speech and the wealthy have more money, then political influence becomes proportional to wealth rather than the 'one person, one vote' ideal — Critics argued this distorts democratic representation in favor of the economically powerful.

choices.text: Critics argued the decision violated federalism by preempting state campaign finance laws
choices.explanation: While Citizens United did affect state campaign finance laws, the primary democratic critique was not about federalism — it was about whether unlimited money in elections distorts democratic equality regardless of which level of government is affected.

choices.text: Critics argued the decision was wrong because corporations are not citizens and should not have any constitutional rights
choices.explanation: While this argument has been made, the primary democratic theory concern is more nuanced — even accepting that corporations have some First Amendment rights, critics argue the equation of unlimited spending with protected speech distorts democratic equality in ways that harm representative government.

questions.subject: ap-government
questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would most likely result in a 'critical election' or political realignment?

choices.text: An incumbent president wins reelection with a margin of 5 percentage points over a strong challenger
choices.explanation: An incumbent reelection by a moderate margin maintains the existing partisan balance — critical elections and realignments require dramatic and lasting shifts in coalition composition, not narrow incumbent victories.

choices.text: A sudden economic crisis, social upheaval, or major policy failure that discredits the existing majority party and drives durable coalition change
choices.explanation: Critical elections historically occur during periods of significant crisis — the Great Depression produced the New Deal coalition of 1932, and the Civil Rights era reshaped party coalitions. A traumatic event that discredits existing leaders and permanently shifts which groups align with which parties produces realignment.

choices.text: A third-party candidate wins 15% of the popular vote in a presidential election but no electoral votes
choices.explanation: A strong third-party performance can foreshadow realignment but does not constitute it — realignment requires lasting shifts in the major party coalitions that persist across multiple elections.

choices.text: A newly elected president appoints several Supreme Court justices who shift the Court's ideological balance
choices.explanation: Court appointments change the judiciary's ideological direction but do not necessarily produce electoral realignment — political realignment is about durable shifts in voter coalitions and party identification among the electorate.

questions.subject: ap-government

stimulus.content: "Priming refers to the media's ability to influence which criteria people use to evaluate political leaders and institutions. When the media focuses intensely on the economy, voters tend to evaluate the president on economic performance. When it focuses on foreign policy, voters shift to evaluating leadership on that dimension."

questions.question: Which of the following is the most significant democratic concern raised by the priming effect described in the passage?

choices.text: Priming could allow media outlets to systematically advantage incumbents by focusing coverage on policy areas where they perform well
choices.explanation: If media can shift which criteria voters use to evaluate leaders, then media organizations (or those who influence them) could strategically prime evaluations to favor or disadvantage specific politicians — this raises concerns about media power to shape electoral outcomes independent of voter preferences.

choices.text: Priming proves that citizens are incapable of forming independent political judgments without media guidance
choices.explanation: Priming describes influence on evaluative criteria, not the elimination of citizen judgment — voters still form their own assessments on primed dimensions. Priming affects which issues are salient, not whether citizens can think independently.

choices.text: Priming effects only occur in authoritarian systems where the government controls media content
choices.explanation: Priming is well-documented in free media environments — experimental research by Iyengar and Kinder demonstrated priming effects in the United States, where the government does not control media content.

choices.text: Priming shows that media bias in favor of one political party inevitably produces one-party rule
